pair.title: Show HN: Mediator.ai – Using Nash bargaining and LLMs to systematize fairness

story.title: Mediator.ai - Cooperative negotiation is a solvable problem

comments.text:
This is a really smart application of bargaining theory to a problem that genuinely matters. The insight that both parties avoid the conversation because they fear signaling distrust is the real bottleneck, and using a neutral intermediary to work through the math privately before surfacing a creative solution addresses that perfectly.

The $10,000 reframing is elegant because it's not splitting the difference or trying to be "fair" in the abstract sense. It's acknowledging the concrete asymmetry in runway (Ben keeps $200k, Priya keeps $2k) while preserving the 70/30 equity split they agreed on. That's the kind of thing a mediator should find: not compromise, but a Pareto improvement that neither party thought to propose.

Reminds me of how Fisher and Ury positioned this in Getting to Yes - the goal isn't to split the pie more fairly, it's to expand the pie so both sides can win on what actually matters to them. Here, what mattered was security for Priya and confidence for Ben that this wasn't going to blow up the friendship later.

Curious how you handle cases where the two sides have genuinely opposed interests though (not just

kids.text:
This is exactly the kind of problem that shouldn't require a neutral third party to solve, but practically always does. The asymmetry isn't just financial, it's psychological. Ben has optionality; Priya doesn't. So any proposal from either of them lands differently, gets interpreted through that lens of desperation vs comfort.

What's clever here is that Mediator isn't splitting the difference or imposing some abstract fairness rule. It's surfacing the $10,000 by actually grinding through the math from both sides' constraints at once. That's harder than it sounds. Most mediators (human ones especially) paper over this with "compromise" language. The algorithm doesn't have ego invested in being seen as wise.

The income-based mortgage split that recalculates yearly is the part that actually scales beyond this one deal. That could become standard for any joint purchase where incomes are unequal. Right now couples either do 50/50 (ignoring reality) or have the awkward conversation about proportional

comments.text:
The fairness framing here is a bit off. "Fair" to whom, and by what standard?

The $10k transfer assumes Priya's lower liquid savings is somehow a deficit to correct before equity calculations. But if Ben had $200k sitting around while Priya scraped together her contribution, that's already priced into what each can afford to put down. Adjusting for it after the fact treats savings accumulation as a moral problem.

The real question is simpler: what happens to the house

kids.text: I think the whole framing assumes there's a neutral mathematical answer to "fair," which there isn't. The article presents Mediator as this objective arbiter surfacing the one true solution, but it's really just encoding one particular philosophy about what counts as a deficit worth correcting. Another mediator using different

> it's really just encoding one particular philosophy about what counts as a deficit worth correcting

Exactly right, but I think that's actually what makes the tool useful rather than a flaw. The point isn't that Mediator found "the" fair answer-it's that it forced Ben and Priya to make their implicit assumptions explicit and then explored the design space systematically. They could have rejected the $10k idea entirely.

What's typically missing in these negotiations is the round-tripping: you propose something, the other person counters, you counter back, and you're both optimizing locally without seeing if there's a Pareto improvement hiding in a different axis entirely. Mediator seems to be automating that exploration-generating dozens of variants and scoring them

I actually worked through a similar situation with a business partner, so I ran the math on this one.

The parent's critique assumes the $10k is post-hoc correction, but I think that's reading it backwards. The real insight here is that Priya's $54k contribution isn't actually 30% of the $180k down payment in any economically meaningful sense. She's putting in her entire liquid net worth. Ben is putting in ~6% of his liquid net worth. The ratio of "financial stress" or "opportunity cost" is nowhere near 70/30.

Standard equity splits usually assume both parties have roughly equal cushion. When they don't, you either (a) pretend they do and resent each other, or (b) adjust upfront.

I tested this with a spreadsheet model: if you calculate what each person could have done with their money instead (Ben's $126k in index funds, Priya's $54k in savings bonds or whatever), the $10k transfer gets you pretty close to "we both sacrificed the same

comments.text:
I've built a few co-ownership agreements in the early 2000s, and this reminds me of something we eventually learned the hard way: the fairness insight here is solid, but the implementation assumes both parties stay rational and cooperative forever.

The $10,000 transfer is elegant on paper. In practice, what happens when one person wants out in year 3? Or when one's income drops 40% and the 65/35 mortgage split suddenly feels impossible? The yearly recalculation is smart, but it's also a yearly renegotiation trigger. I've seen couples fight harder over the *mechanism* for recalculating splits than they fought over the original number.

The real win here isn't the bargaining algorithm, it's forcing both people to articulate what they actually need before drafting anything. That conversation is the hard part. Most people skip it because it feels confrontational. Mediator makes it procedural instead of emotional. That's where the

kids.text:
This feels like the classic HN problem: we've optimized for the happy path and called it solved. The article is doing what a lot of bargaining-theory content does these days-it name-drops game theory and mechanism design, surfaces one elegant edge case (the $10k transfer), and then stops.

The parent nails the real issue. You can generate mathematically fair agreements all day, but fairness on paper and fairness *in practice* are different things. Once the agreement is signed, you've got two people with misaligned incentives and no way to enforce the "spirit" of what you both agreed to.

What actually happens: someone's life changes. Income drops, job loss, divorce, health crisis. Now that 65/35 split that made perfect sense in January feels catastrophic to one party. Do they renegotiate? Do they quietly resent it? Do they lawyer up? The algorithm can't predict which.

The yearly recalculation mentioned is supposed to solve this, but that's where the rubber meets the road. You're asking two co-owners to sit down once a year, rerun the fairness calculation, and potentially redistribute equity based on changed circumstances. That's... actually hard. It's a conversation people avoid having.

I'd be curious whether Mediator has any data on what happens post-agreement. Do people actually reneg

I keep thinking about the 1990s co-op housing movements in Portland and Seattle. They worked until they didn't, always at the moment someone needed to cash out early or a job disappeared. The fairness math was bulletproof. The human part was where it fell apart. This feels like we're

We actually built something similar for co-op housing agreements a few years back. The core insight about iterative scoring is right, but we found the real friction point wasn't the math-it was enforcement when circumstances shifted. We ended up bolting on a lightweight arbitration layer (not lawyers, just a structured process

comments.text:
The bargaining-theory angle is genuinely novel here, but I'm curious about the actual mechanism. The framing suggests Mediator is running something like Nash bargaining or Kalai-Smorodinsky to find Pareto improvements, but the writeup doesn't explain how it avoids the classic problem: both parties have incentive to misrepresent their BATNA (best alternative to negotiated agreement) during the "private walk-through."

If Ben knows Priya will see his

kids.text:
This is a neat demo of the "find the deal both sides missed" pattern, but it glosses over something important: the entire value of the system depends on honest input during the private phase. And there's no mechanism here that actually enforces honesty.

The parent hits the real problem. Ben can tell Mediator "my BATNA is walking away, I have plenty of other options" when that's false, or Priya can understate her savings to make the algorithm tilt toward protecting her downside. The writeup doesn't address how the system detects or prevents this.

Mediator.ai frames this as a neutral tool, but that framing itself is the issue. A truly neutral bargaining mechanism (like sealed-bid auctions, or some commitment device that makes lying costly) needs teeth. This looks like it's relying on user goodwill and post-hoc honesty checks.

The other thing that bugs me: this feels like it should be a regulatory category. Two people co-buying a house and dividing ownership have legal rights and obligations that mediation software can't override. You can't contract around fiduciary duty or equitable distribution in most jurisdictions. Mediator

I tried to game this out with a mediator tool last year on a co-founder equity split. The BATNA misrepresentation problem is real - I told the tool I'd walk if I didn't get 40%, but I would've accepted 35%. My co-founder probably did something similar

This whole category of tools reminds me of the early mediation software from the 90s - Cybersettle, eSpeed's negotiation platform. They all hit the same wall: the moment money or equity is on the table, rational actors shade their truthfulness. I spent 2015-2017 helping

comments.text:
The framing here is doing interesting work. This isn't really about negotiation or bargaining theory in the abstract - it's about making visible what was previously invisible. Ben and Priya were stuck because the conversation itself felt adversarial. Mediator resets that by moving the problem from "how do we divide what we have" to "what does fair actually look like given our constraints?"

The $10,000 transfer only makes sense once you acknowledge the asymmetry in their starting positions. Without that reframe, both of them are trapped in "70/30 split the down payment" math, which appears neutral but encodes Ben's liquidity advantage into the equity structure forever.

What's clever is that this exact move - making hidden asymmetries explicit, then solving for fairness - is exactly

The insight here is that both parties were stuck in a coordination problem, not a disagreement about fairness. Ben and Priya both wanted the same outcome (a written agreement that feels fair), but neither could propose it without triggering the other's defensive read.

What Mediator does is separate the *generation* phase from the *evaluation* phase. Instead of each person proposing and defending a position (which signals distrust), an algorithm generates candidates that satisfy both sets of constraints, then scores them. The $10,000 transfer isn't something either of them would have proposed solo, because proposing

kids.text: This reminds me of the "fair division" problem from the 1950s, Steinhaus and Banach worked on cake-cutting protocols that separate proposal from evaluation. The fact that it took until 2024 for someone to apply that framing to real estate negotiations is kind of wild.

I looked into the Steinhaus-Banach cake-cutting work after reading this, and the parallel is real but the jump to real estate is trickier than it sounds.

The cake-cutting protocols work because the "cake" is infinitely divisible and the payoff is continuous. A house isn't. You can't give Priya 35.7% of the mortgage payment in a way that feels natural, you either split it or you don't. The novelty here isn't really the fair-division framing (that's old), it's that Mediator is doing something closer to constraint satisfaction: generate candidates that satisfy *both* people's hard constraints (Ben needs X liquidity, Priya needs Y security), then score them against soft preferences (fairness, simplicity, precedent).

The $10,000 solution works because it's not trying to divide the house itself. It's introducing a new variable (a transfer outside the equity split) that wasn't on the table when Ben and Priya were stuck. That's more like Schelling point discovery than fair division.

I'd be curious whether Mediator actually uses bargaining theory (as the headline claims) or whether it's running a genetic algorithm or constraint solver. The writeup doesn't specify

This reads like a solution desperately searching for a problem. Ben and Priya didn't need an AI to figure out a $10k transfer. They needed to have an uncomfortable conversation, which is what actually builds trust in a partnership.

The gotcha is right there in your own framing: "neither could propose it without triggering the other's defensive read." So instead of facing that dynamic, they outsourced it to an algorithm. Now they can both point at Mediator and say "the math said so" instead of actually negotiating. That's not cooperation, that's conflict avoidance with extra steps.

And the $10k answer? It's not some breakthrough from bargaining theory. It's the obvious move any competent mediator (the human kind) would surface in 20 minutes. Priya's broke, Ben's flush, fairness suggests a transfer. The fact that they

I went through something similar when my partner and I bought our first place together. We had wildly different savings (I had about $80k, she had $12k), and we both wanted to split the mortgage fairly but couldn't figure out how without one of us feeling like they were subsidizing the other's lifestyle choices before we even closed.

We tried the obvious stuff first: straight 50/50, then income-based splits, then some hybrid thing where we'd adjust year-to-year. Every proposal felt like a negotiation tactic rather than a solution, which made the whole thing tense.

What eventually worked was having a mediator (a real person, not an AI) suggest something neither of us had proposed: I covered the down payment gap that reflected our different savings rates, she handled more of the closing costs, and then we split ongoing payments by income going forward. It wasn't a math trick. It was just... acknowledging that fairness isn't the same as equality, and that the initial wealth gap was real and needed to be addressed separately from the ongoing partnership.

The Mediator.ai framing here resonates because
